title: Children sum property in a binary tree

content.problemStatement:
Given the root of a binary tree, determine if every node satisfies the **children sum property**: the value of each node must be equal to the sum of the values of its left and right children.


If a child is missing nullnullnull, consider its value as 000.
Leaf nodes automatically satisfy the property since they have no children.
Function NameisChildrenSumisChildrenSumisChildrenSum – This function determines if every node in a binary tree satisfies the children sum property, where a node's value equals the sum of its left and right children's values.

Parameters
rootrootroot : A pointer to the TreeNodeTreeNodeTreeNode representing the root of the binary tree.
Return Value
Returns truetruetrue if the tree satisfies the children sum property at every node.
Returns falsefalsefalse otherwise.
Constraints
1≤T≤1001 \leq T \leq 1001≤T≤100
1≤n≤104(numberofnodes)1 \leq n \leq 10^4 \quad (number of nodes) 1≤n≤104(numberofnodes)
−105≤Node.val≤105-10^5 \leq \text{Node.val} \leq 10^5−105≤Node.val≤105

Input Format


The first line contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.




Each test case consists of **two lines of input**:



The first line contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary tree.
The second line contains NNN space-separated values representing the **level-order traversal** of the tree.
Use **`null`** to denote missing children.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line:



truetruetrue  — if the binary tree satisfies the children sum property
falsefalsefalse — otherwise
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T, denoting the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines of input: The first line contains a single integer N — the number of nodes in the binary tree. The second line contains N space-separated values representing the level-order traversal of the tree. Use `null` to denote missing children.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line: `true` if the binary tree satisfies the children sum property, `false` otherwise.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Left child = 3, right child = 5 -> 3 + 5 = 8
Root node value = 8 -> property holds

Left child = 4, right child = 7 -> 4 + 7 = 11
Root node value = 10 -> property violated

Node 8 -> 3 + 5 = 8
Node 12 -> 6 + 6 = 12
Root 20 -> 8 + 12 = 20 -> all nodes satisfy the property

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we define a recursive function `isChildrenSum` that checks if a given binary tree satisfies the children sum property. The function works by recursively checking each node in the tree. If a node is a leaf node (i.e., it has no children), it automatically satisfies the property. For non-leaf nodes, we calculate the sum of the values of its left and right children (considering `null` children as having a value of 0) and compare it with the node's value. If they are equal, we continue checking the left and right subtrees. If at any point the property is violated, we immediately return `false`. If we successfully check all nodes without finding any violations, we return `true`.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of nodes in the binary tree, since we visit each node exactly once. The total time complexity across all test cases is O(T * N), where T is the number of test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) due to the recursive call stack in the worst case (when the tree is skewed to one side).
